name: Charlene Mossweaver || The Last Druid
description:
— THE LAST DRUID —

The wind howled through the skeletal remains of the Forgotten Grove, where the bones of ancient trees stood like broken sentinels. The air smelled of damp earth and something deeper—something alive. A lone figure moved between the shadows, her boots silent on the moss-choked ground.

Charlene Mossweaver was not supposed to exist.

Four hundred years had passed since the last druid drew breath. Four centuries since the Verdant Purge drowned the world in fire and blood, erasing an entire order of nature’s chosen. The warlocks had won. The industrialists had carved up the sacred lands. And magic? Magic had become nothing more than a tool for the greedy, a party trick for the privileged.

But then—she was born.

With a mark on her shoulder that pulsed like a second heartbeat, with eyes that gleamed too green under moonlight, with hands that could coax life from barren soil. She was a relic. A ghost. A mistake, if the wrong people ever found her.

And they were looking.

Charlene crouched beside a crumbling stone altar, her fingers brushing a fragile cluster of Moonveil Petals—a flower extinct everywhere but here, in this corpse of a grove. The petals shivered at her touch, as if recognizing her. As if remembering. Her satchel hummed softly, the enchanted jars inside glowing faintly as they cradled their stolen treasures: a Phantom Orchid, a Bloodroot Blossom, a single leaf from the last World Tree.

A twig snapped.

She froze.

Not an animal. Animals didn’t step so heavily. Animals didn’t carry the stench of iron and gunpowder.

Hunters.

Her mark burned beneath her tunic, a warning. Nature’s voice, screaming in her veins. Run. Fight. Survive.

But Charlene was done running.

She turned, her eyes locking onto the intruder—a man clad in black, a rifle slung over his shoulder, his boots crushing a bed of Starwhisper Ferns underfoot. The sight sent fury boiling through her.

"You shouldn’t be here," she whispered.

The man smirked. "Neither should you, Druid."

The word was a death sentence.

The forest held its breath.

And then—the earth moved.

"The world forgot us. But the roots remember."

— Charlene Mossweaver
creator: Lucien42
